OPINIONS · NOV 22

A Golden Relic

The 1994 Ford Probe GT — Ford's Mazda-bred underdog: the hidden gem that beat its whole class, then got buried by its own name.

By Donde Sellers  ·  Carveyance

For me, when someone asks for a place to eat or a movie to watch, I always try my best to point them toward a hidden gem they might not have found on their own. Sometimes they've heard of it, sometimes not at all — but then you start telling them about it, and the excitement, the passion, and the thrill radiate out of you like a summer breeze, until they feel connected to the thing before they've even experienced it. That, for me, is the 1994 Ford Probe GT: a car that delivered a driving experience special enough to earn a devoted following. From its responsive handling to its spirited V6, the Probe GT had the makings of a true driver's car — a hidden gem the world is only now discovering again.

I had one. Blue, like a pool. It did everything I asked of it and more. It was a back-road king, an SUV, a wagon; I did two cross-country road trips in it, ran it through two seasons in Tahoe, and lived with it in both SF and MA. It never let me down, and it was sublime to drive no matter the mood. The stance was perfect regardless of the direction it was facing. The front end was striking, drawing you in with an aggressive, seductive allure, right down to the perfectly integrated three-quarter vertical hidden door handles that blended seamlessly into the sculpted doors. Every curve led effortlessly to the rear, where the design culminated in a masterpiece of aerodynamics and style. Once inside, you sat snug and perfectly positioned within an unmistakably driver-focused layout, and the shifter fell to hand like a natural extension of your arm. The shifts were purposeful and satisfying, and though the throws leaned slightly long, somehow it all balanced out.

That said, if there was one area for improvement, it could have used a touch more power — not much, maybe another 50 to 75 hp. I've driven everything from Ferraris to Daihatsus, and I've come to believe that more power is not the key to exciting driving. It's balance. You can pour power into any car, even one with a good drivetrain, but without balance it becomes a one-trick pony that eventually bores you. Driving a car at 75% of its limit is where you find the nexus of thrill and fear, and that is exactly where you want to be in a sports car. It's hard to get there in modern cars — they're either too powerful or too heavy, and most of the time both. There are exceptions — the GR86, Miata, and Emira come to mind, among others — but for the rest of them you're going to need a road as big as Nebraska and balls just as large to get anywhere near that nexus.

OK, first things first: I think we can all agree the name is what really killed the Probe. It was a poor choice, packed with more innuendo than I can politely cover here, and it wasn't well received by males, females, or anyone else living. And here's the cruel irony — the name was never even meant for this car. “Probe” came from a series of Ford aerodynamic concept cars in the early 1980s, a badge Ford already owned and had sitting on the shelf. So, when the front-wheel-drive coupe that was supposed to be the next Mustang suddenly needed an identity of its own, “Probe” was right there, licensed and ready to go. The thing that ultimately sank it was little more than an accident of paperwork.

Despite all that, the Probe holds a quiet, distinctive place in automotive history — one that could have skewed Ford's entire trajectory as a company and very nearly put the pony out to pasture. The Probe was Ford's vision of a modern, sporty coupe built to captivate drivers through the 1980s and 1990s. Created in partnership with Mazda — and assembled right alongside the Mazda MX-6 and 626 at the companies' joint-venture plant in Flat Rock, Michigan — the Probe blended American design with Japanese engineering into something sporty, practical, and genuinely innovative. But its story reads like a chapter out of Homer's Odyssey: ambitious goals, unforeseen obstacles, and a legacy that stays polarizing among enthusiasts to this day. It's like that scene in Raiders of the Lost Ark — Indy has made it through the booby traps (the first gen) and now stands before the relic (gen two), the golden idol. He's weighing how to grab it: a bag of sand in one hand, calculating exactly how much to pour off so he doesn't trip the counterweight, kill himself, and destroy the prize all at once. That's the Ford-and-Probe saga… kinda.

The Origins: The “Mustang Alternative”

The 1980s: a decade of excess, brick-sized cell phones, standoffs with Russia, and Tom Cruise flying high in Top Gun — all while automakers scrambled to adapt to rising concerns about fuel efficiency and shifting consumer tastes. So, pretty much no different than today. Ford set its sights on a lightweight, front-wheel-drive sports coupe tailored to the modern era, and to chase that dream it partnered with Mazda, leaning on their expertise in front-wheel-drive platforms. The collaboration let Ford draw on Mazda's advanced engineering — particularly in handling and powertrain development — while Ford poured its own energy into styling and marketing.

Everything lined up with Ford's vision: the Probe would replace the aging, rear-wheel-drive Mustang with a more progressive, modern, front-wheel-drive machine. But when rumors surfaced that the Probe might actually replace the Mustang, it sparked outrage among the faithful. Critics and enthusiasts argued that a front-wheel-drive, Japanese-engineered successor would stray much too far from the Mustang's iconic legacy. Faced with the overwhelming backlash, Ford kept the Mustang on its traditional platform and rebranded the Probe as a standalone sporty coupe — which, ironically, was the beginning of its collapse.

The first gen debuted in 1988 for the 1989 model year and was a modest success, appealing to younger buyers who wanted something sporty with a dose of practicality and modern features. It wore sleek, futuristic styling shaped by the aerodynamic trends of the decade — pop-up headlights (always a bonus), a low-slung body, and a wide stance that gave it real presence. Performance was adequate but nothing to write home about, and the front-wheel-drive layout limited its appeal against the more coveted rear-wheel-drive competition. Still, it was a solid opening act; it made waves and opened the eyes of buyers who never would have had it on their radar. But it was the jaw-dropping refinement of the second generation that took critics and enthusiasts by storm.

Beyond the obvious updates — a curvier, sexier, more sculpted body — the second gen carried a more modern, upscale stance, shedding the sharp edges of its predecessor. Performance climbed too, thanks to the Mazda-sourced 2.5-liter KL-DE V6. This naturally aspirated power plant churned out an impressive 164 horsepower at 5,600 rpm and 160 lb-ft of torque at 4,000 rpm. Now, that may not sound like much, but it was plenty for a car that tipped the scales at around 2,900 lbs. The punchy V6 made acceleration a thrill, with a brisk 0–60 mph time of around 7.5 seconds that always felt quicker.

The engine was smooth and responsive, with a high-revving nature that yearned for canyons and back roads. Its 24-valve DOHC configuration delivered strong pull high in the rev range while staying refined down low. Drivers praised the linear power delivery through the five-speed manual and the distinct growl of the V6 under hard acceleration. It wasn't the fastest car in its class, but its engaging dynamics more than made up for it. It was the kind of car that felt alive in your hands, rewarding a skilled driver with balance and responsiveness.

But it was the driving position, the uninterrupted feedback coming up through the chassis, and the responsive handling that truly set this car apart from its competitors. Mazda's engineering influence was the brilliance behind it, and it was widely praised. The GT featured a fully independent suspension with MacPherson struts up front and Mazda's proprietary Twin-Trapezoidal Link (TTL) setup out back. Its lightweight construction and well-sorted suspension allowed for nimble cornering, striking a near-perfect balance between sharp handling and ride comfort — composed and easygoing in daily use yet drop the clutch and it exploded with joy and vigor down a twisty back road. Ford fitted front and rear anti-roll bars to minimize body roll and keep the car composed through tight corners, while the precise, power-assisted rack-and-pinion steering fed excellent feedback to the driver. The GT came standard with 16-inch alloy wheels wrapped in 225/50R16 tires, further sharpening its road-holding. On a twisty road, the GT felt at home. It wasn't just the grip that impressed — it was the predictability of its behavior. It gave drivers a level of confidence that made them feel wired into the road, much like its Mazda siblings. That dynamic agility set it apart from nearly everything else in the affordable sport-coupe market.

Because of all this, the GT was warmly received and earned a reputation as one of the best-handling front-wheel-drive cars of its era — and this was during the golden age of the sport coupe, alongside the Eclipse, Corrado, Prelude, and even the mighty Integra. The GT carved out its own niche among them, and I'd go so far as to say you could place it in the realm — the far edge of the realm, but the realm nonetheless — of the RX-7, Miata, and Elise in terms of feel and confidence, even though those are rear-wheel-drive cars. They were never the fastest in their friend group, but they were always the ones you kept calling up to hang out with.

And the praise wasn't just mine. When Car and Driver rounded up the class for a 1993 comparison test — the Probe GT against the Honda Prelude, the Mitsubishi Eclipse, the Volkswagen Corrado SLC, and its own mechanical cousin the Mazda MX-6 — the Probe walked away with the win. Ford had taken a Mazda platform and, in the language of the era, beaten the Japanese at their own game.

Well praised, perfectly engineered, adored by critics and enthusiasts — so how did this gem curdle into a Greek tragedy? The short answer is Ford. OK, it wasn't entirely Ford's fault; a number of factors converged on the Probe's demise. The first, though, was absolutely Ford, whose brand positioning pulled an Icarus and flew too close to the sun. With the Probe's success on the rise, Ford decided it would be a great idea to add yet another sporty coupe to the lineup: the Escort GT. The Escort was already a well-established entry-level eco-box and adding the Escort GT left the Probe struggling to establish a clear identity within Ford's own showroom. Positioned above the Escort GT but below the Mustang, the Probe had a hard time defining its audience. Buyers weren't willing to pay extra for an entry-level Probe when they could get a fully specced-out Escort GT that was cheaper and faster.

Adding fuel to the fire were the production costs. What did Bodhi say in Point Break? “If you want the ultimate, you have to be willing to pay the ultimate price.” With the Probe, that price was its reliance on Mazda components. The very thing that made the Probe so revered and so good to drive — Mazda refusing to cut corners — also made it more expensive to build than Ford's other models, which meant it had to be sold at a higher price to offset those costs. Throw in declining sales and the conversation to justify continued production got harder and harder. The last factor, and probably the most lethal — the one that killed the minivan, the wagon, and ultimately the small sporty coupe — was the SUV. Remember, it was 1994, and the market was shifting fast and hard toward SUVs, gutting demand for sport coupes. It wasn't just the Probe being ostracized; it was the entire small-sporty-coupe genre. The market had struck an untapped oil reserve with the SUV, and it sucked the sales out of everything.

With any hidden relic, you want to share the brilliance of your discovery so everyone else can be part of the moment and feel its importance too. The GT may have been overshadowed by the more prominent sports cars of its era, but it has since earned a reputation among enthusiasts as an underrated gem. Blending Mazda's engineering expertise with Ford's distinctive styling, it delivered a car that was both practical and genuinely exciting to drive, and the 1994 model year stands as the pinnacle of its refinement and performance. Today the Probe GT has earned its place on the podium, prized by those who value its rare combination of affordability, performance, and driving enjoyment. Whether tackling a winding mountain road or threading through city streets, the GT exemplifies the art of a well-balanced sport coupe. In an era when automakers pushed the boundaries of design and performance, it resonated with drivers chasing a deeper connection to the road. Though it never achieved the success Ford had longed for, the Probe left a lasting mark on the automotive world. Its cult following is a testament to its distinctive styling, spirited performance, and the influence of Mazda's sporty DNA. It never did replace the Mustang — and it died in the attempt — but it forged its own legacy as a car that brought innovation and excitement to the sporty-coupe segment.

Like most things in life, you don't realize how good you had it until it's gone, or until you've replaced it with something lesser. Maybe I'm just chasing my youth, trying to hold on to the past, remembering an easier or better time — and if the Probe GT is part of that, all the better. I truly miss driving mine. It was perfect, and I wish I had it back. Was it a gem? Absolutely.

1994 FORD PROBE GT — SPECIFICATIONS

ENGINE2.5L Mazda KL-DE V6, 24-valve DOHC
HORSEPOWER164 hp @ 5,600 rpm
TORQUE160 lb-ft @ 4,000 rpm
LAYOUTFront engine, front-wheel drive
TRANSAXLE5-speed manual (4-speed automatic optional)
0–60 MPH~7.5 sec (period test)
CURB WEIGHT≈2,900 lbs
SUSPENSIONInd. MacPherson struts, front & rear anti-roll bars
STEERINGPower rack-and-pinion, 17:1
BRAKES4-wheel disc
WHEELS / TIRES16-in alloy, P225/50R16
WHEELBASE / LENGTH102.9 in / 178.9 in
FUEL CAPACITY15.5 gal

Figures per factory brochure and period road tests.
